title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle (Fragment 2)
text:
“If you’re going to be away long,” he said, “you might leave some logs where I can reach them.”

“ Can you pick up logs?” Sophie asked, intrigued in spite of her impatience.

For answer, Calcifer stretched out a blue arm-shaped flame divided into green fingerlike flames at the end. It was not very long, nor did it look strong. “See? I can almost reach the hearth,” he said proudly.

Sophie stacked a pile of logs in front of the grate so that Calcifer could at least reach the top one. “You’re not to burn them until you’ve got them in the grate,” she warned him, and she set off for the door yet again.

This time somebody knocked on it before she got there.

It was one of those days, Sophie thought. It must be the sea captain. She put up her hand to turn the knob blue-down.

“No, it’s the castle door,” Calcifer said. “But I’m not sure—”

Then it was Michael back for some reason, Sophie thought as she opened the door.

A turnip face leered at her. She smelled mildew. Against the wide blue sky, a ragged arm ending in the stump of a stick wheeled round and tried to paw at her. It was a scarecrow. It was only made of sticks and rags, but it was alive, and it was trying to come in.

“Calcifer!” Sophie screamed. “Make the castle go faster!”

The stone blocks round the doorway crunched and grated. The green-brown moorland was suddenly rushing past. The scarecrow’s stick arm thumped on the door, and then went scraping along the wall of the castle as the castle left it behind. It wheeled its other arm round and seemed to try to clutch at the stonework. It meant to get into the castle if it could.

Sophie slammed the door shut. This, she thought, just showed how stupid it was for an eldest child to try to seek her fortune! That was the scarecrow she had propped in the hedge on her way to the castle. She had made jokes to it. Now, as if her jokes had brought it to evil life, it had followed her all the way here and tried to paw at her face. She ran to the window to see if the thing was still trying to get into the castle.

Of course, all she could see was a sunny day in Porthaven, with a dozen sails going up a dozen masts beyond the roofs opposite, and a cloud of seagulls circling in the blue sky.

“That’s the difficulty of being in several places at once!” Sophie said to the human skull on the bench.

Then, all at once, she discovered the real drawback to being an old woman. Her heart gave a leap and a little stutter, and then seemed to be trying to bang its way out of her chest. It hurt. She shook all over and her knees trembled. She rather thought she might be dying. It was all she could do to get to the chair by the hearth. She sat their panting, clutching her chest.

“Is something the matter?” Calcifer asked.

“Yes. My heart. There was a scarecrow at the door!” Sophie gasped.

“What has a scarecrow to do with your heart?” Calcifer asked.

“It was trying to get in here. It gave me a terrible fright. And my heart—but you wouldn’t understand, you silly young demon!” Sophie panted. “You haven’t got a heart.”

“Yes I have,” Calcifer said, as proudly as he had revealed his arm. “Down in the glowing part under the logs. And don’t call me young. I’m a good million years older than you are! Can I reduce the speed of the castle now?”

“Only if the scarecrow’s gone,” said Sophie. “Has it?”

“I can’t tell,” said Calcifer. “It’s not flesh and blood, you see. I told you I couldn’t really see outside.”

Sophie got up and dragged herself to the door again, feeling ill. She opened it slowly and cautiously. Green steepness, rocks, and purple slopes whirled past, making her feel dizzy, but she took a grip on the door frame and leaned out to look along the wall to the moorland they were leaving behind. The scarecrow was about fifty yards to the rear. It was hopping from clump to heather clump with a sinister sort of valiance, holding its fluttering stick arms at an angle to balance it on the hillside. As Sophie watched, the castle left it further behind. It was slow, but it was still following. She shut the door.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle

vocabulary.definition: To grasp or hold tightly or eagerly.
vocabulary.example: It seemed to try to clutch at the stonework.

vocabulary.definition: Grasping tightly or eagerly.
vocabulary.example: She sat panting, clutching her chest.

vocabulary.definition: To make a loud crushing sound while being crushed or being walked on.
vocabulary.example: The stone blocks round the doorway crunched and grated.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling as if the surroundings are spinning.
vocabulary.example: Green steepness, rocks, and purple slopes whirled past, making her feel dizzy.

vocabulary.definition: Moving lightly and quickly; waving.
vocabulary.example: Holding its fluttering stick arms at an angle.

vocabulary.definition: A frame of metal bars for holding fuel in a fireplace.
vocabulary.example: You’re not to burn them until you’ve got them in the grate.

vocabulary.definition: To make a harsh, scraping sound.
vocabulary.example: The stone blocks round the doorway crunched and grated.

vocabulary.definition: To chop or cut (something, especially wood) with an axe, pick, or other tool.
vocabulary.example: (Not in text, omit)

vocabulary.definition: The feeling of not wanting to wait.
vocabulary.example: Intrigued in spite of her impatience.

vocabulary.definition: Interested and curious about something.
vocabulary.example: Sophie asked, intrigued in spite of her impatience.

vocabulary.definition: A rounded handle, especially on a door.
vocabulary.example: She put up her hand to turn the knob blue-down.

ru.translation: пожирать взглядом, смотреть с неприличным интересом

vocabulary.definition: Look or gaze in an unpleasant, malicious, or lascivious way.
vocabulary.example: A turnip face leered at her.

vocabulary.definition: A fungus that grows on damp organic matter, causing decay.
vocabulary.example: She smelled mildew.

vocabulary.definition: An open area of hills covered with rough grass and heather.
vocabulary.example: The green-brown moorland was suddenly rushing past.

vocabulary.definition: To touch or handle clumsily or roughly, especially with the paws or hands.
vocabulary.example: It wheeled round and tried to paw at her.

vocabulary.definition: Supported by placing something beneath or against it.
vocabulary.example: That was the scarecrow she had propped in the hedge on her way to the castle.

vocabulary.definition: Worn out or torn, in bad condition.
vocabulary.example: A ragged arm ending in the stump of a stick wheeled round.

vocabulary.definition: Moved very quickly in a particular direction.
vocabulary.example: The green-brown moorland was suddenly rushing past.

vocabulary.definition: An object made to look like a human figure that is put in fields to scare birds away.
vocabulary.example: It was a scarecrow. It was only made of sticks and rags, but it was alive.

vocabulary.definition: Suggesting or threatening evil or harm.
vocabulary.example: With a sinister sort of valiance.

vocabulary.definition: To close something noisily and forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Sophie slammed the door shut.

vocabulary.definition: To arrange objects in a neat pile.
vocabulary.example: Sophie stacked a pile of logs in front of the grate so that Calcifer could at least reach the top one.

vocabulary.definition: A small remaining part, especially of a limb or tree.
vocabulary.example: A ragged arm ending in the stump of a stick wheeled round.

vocabulary.definition: Courage or bravery, especially in battle.
vocabulary.example: It was hopping from clump to heather clump with a sinister sort of valiance.

vocabulary.definition: To turn around quickly.
vocabulary.example: A ragged arm wheeled round and tried to paw at her.
